Triggers.value: Night-time or waking up in the dark

Threat.value: I scan for exits as soon as I enter any room or building
I study people's faces and body language to detect if they are a threat
I feel on guard even inside my own home

Memory.value: I remember the frightening event as a few moments, and each moment is a picture in my mind.
My memories of the frightening event are mostly pictures or images.
I can't seem to put the frightening event into words.

Negative Appraisals.value: I feel isolated and set apart from others
I am permanently an outsider now
The event happened because of the way I acted

issues.item: Verbal Access: I can talk about what happened very easily.
issues.explanation: This conflicts with the severe PTSD profile and with the other memory items, which describe distressing, intrusive, fragmented trauma memory. Very easy verbal access to the trauma is hard to reconcile with a PCL-5 of 80 and the rest of the formulation.

issues.item: Story Feels Unreal: The memory is so extreme it is hard to believe it actually happened to me
issues.explanation: This can fit trauma-related dissociation, but it sits awkwardly with 'I can talk about what happened very easily' within the same memory node. Together they suggest opposing memory states without a clear clinical bridge.
This is somewhat broad, but it is clinically coherent with terrorism-related trauma and the high-threat formulation. No issue.

vignette:
Rajan, a 58-year-old South Asian plumber, presents with severe post-traumatic stress symptoms that flare most sharply at night, around bang-like sounds, and whenever he feels he is not in control. He describes a terrorism incident in which a sudden explosion shattered the scene and, in the peak moment of danger, he froze, unable to do much beyond trying to get clear of the danger. Even now, waking in the dark or hearing a sharp bang from tools, pipes, or a slammed van door can make him feel as if the danger is back in the room.

When Rajan speaks about the trauma, he does not tell it as a flowing story. He breaks it into a few isolated moments, each described like a picture he cannot move away from. He returns to the same blast, the same flash of chaos, and says he cannot put the event into words properly. What comes back to him is visual and fragmentary, with the images arriving without any smooth narrative.

His central stuck point is painfully clear: Rajan feels that the event showed him he is “permanently an outsider now,” and that “it happened because of the way I acted.” He also says he feels isolated and set apart from other people, as though he no longer belongs in the same world as those around him.

In daily life, Rajan scans for exits as soon as he enters a room or building, including client homes and job sites. He studies people’s faces and body language to work out whether they might be a threat, and even inside his own house he says he remains on guard. At night he checks the doors and listens carefully when he wakes in the dark. He is a plumber, so these reactions are visible in other people’s homes, on building sites, and in cramped service spaces where he cannot instantly control who is nearby or where the nearest exit is.

The trauma memory stays in his mind as a few sharp moments, each one like a picture. When he tries to describe it, he becomes stuck on the same image of the blast and the same fragment of chaos, with no clear words to link them together. Night-time darkness, bang sounds, and moments of feeling out of control sit close to that memory for him, and he speaks about them in the same breath as the blast itself. His beliefs about being an outsider and about having acted wrongly are also close to the surface. Rajan’s marriage is affected too: he is physically present but preoccupied and watchful, often distracted from ordinary conversation because he is assessing who is near the door and where he could get out quickly.

vignette_attempts.vignette:
Rajan, a 58-year-old South Asian plumber, presents with severe post-traumatic stress symptoms that flare most sharply at night, around bang-like sounds, and whenever he feels he is not in control. He describes a terrorism incident in which a sudden explosion shattered the scene and, in the peak moment of danger, he could do little more than freeze and try to get himself to safety. Even now, the dark of night or waking unexpectedly in darkness takes him back to that helpless moment, and loud bangs from tools, pipes, or a slammed van door can jolt him into immediate panic.

When Rajan speaks about the trauma, he does not tell it as a flowing story. He breaks it into a few isolated moments, each described as if it were a picture he cannot move away from. He points to the same instant of blast, the same fragment of chaos, and says he cannot put the event into words properly. Instead, it comes back to him in stark images that arrive without transition.

His central stuck point is painfully clear: Rajan feels that the event showed him he is “permanently an outsider now,” and that he is somehow to blame because “it happened because of the way I acted.” He also says he feels isolated and set apart from other people, as if something has changed in him that cannot be reversed.

In daily life, Rajan scans for exits as soon as he enters a room or building, including client homes and job sites. He studies people’s faces and body language to work out whether they might be a threat, and even inside his own house he says he remains on guard. At night he checks the doors and listens for noises when he wakes in the dark. He avoids standing near loud equipment when possible and becomes tense if a sudden bang echoes across a repair site.

A self-reinforcing pattern is evident. Night-time darkness, bang sounds, and moments of feeling out of control bring the trauma back in fragments and images, which intensify his sense that he is unsafe, isolated, and somehow responsible. Those appraisals then push him into more scanning, more vigilance around faces and exits, and more guardedness at home and at work. Rajan’s constant checking keeps the event mentally close, and the repeated return of those pictures and bodily alarms makes the same beliefs feel even more convincing. His marriage is affected too: he is present physically but preoccupied and watchful, often distracted from ordinary conversation because he is assessing who is near the door and where he could get out quickly.

violations.explanation: Absent: while Rajan does scan, check, and remain guarded, the vignette does not explicitly connect triggers causally to a maladaptive strategies component in the required sense, and in any case the graph forbids this edge. No violation marked because there is no explicit causal phrasing in the forbidden direction.

Absent: the vignette says appraisals push him into scanning/vigilance, but because the target component Maladaptive Strategies must not appear and there is no explicit mention of that component, this forbidden edge is not explicitly stated.
Absent: no explicit causal language connects memory to maladaptive strategies as a named component.
Absent: although threat-related fear leads to vigilance behaviors in the narrative, the vignette does not explicitly state the forbidden component relationship in direct causal terms.
Absent: the phrase "keeps the event mentally close" is adjacent to checking, but it does not explicitly state the forbidden component Maladaptive Strategies causing Memory.
Absent: the vignette says repeated return of pictures and bodily alarms makes beliefs more convincing, but this is not an explicit causal statement from Maladaptive Strategies to Negative Appraisals.

No violation marked because there is no explicit causal phrasing in the forbidden direction.
- Negative Appraisals → Maladaptive Strategies: Absent: the vignette says appraisals push him into scanning/vigilance, but because the target component Maladaptive Strategies must not appear and there is no explicit mention of that component, this forbidden edge is not explicitly stated.

- Threat → Maladaptive Strategies: Absent: although threat-related fear leads to vigilance behaviors in the narrative, the vignette does not explicitly state the forbidden component relationship in direct causal terms.
- Maladaptive Strategies → Memory: Absent: the phrase "keeps the event mentally close" is adjacent to checking, but it does not explicitly state the forbidden component Maladaptive Strategies causing Memory.
- Maladaptive Strategies → Negative Appraisals: Absent: the vignette says repeated return of pictures and bodily alarms makes beliefs more convincing, but this is not an explicit causal statement from Maladaptive Strategies to Negative Appraisals.

vignette_attempts.vignette:
Rajan, a 58-year-old South Asian plumber, presents with severe post-traumatic stress symptoms that flare most sharply at night, around bang-like sounds, and whenever he feels he is not in control. He describes a terrorism incident in which a sudden explosion shattered the scene and, in the peak moment of danger, he froze, unable to do much beyond trying to get clear of the danger. Even now, waking in the dark or hearing a sharp bang from tools, pipes, or a slammed van door can make him feel as if the danger is back in the room.

When Rajan speaks about the trauma, he does not tell it as a flowing story. He breaks it into a few isolated moments, each described like a picture he cannot move away from. He returns to the same blast, the same flash of chaos, and says he cannot put the event into words properly. What comes back to him is visual and fragmentary, with the images arriving without any smooth narrative.

His central stuck point is painfully clear: Rajan feels that the event showed him he is “permanently an outsider now,” and that “it happened because of the way I acted.” He also says he feels isolated and set apart from other people, as though he no longer belongs in the same world as those around him.

In daily life, Rajan scans for exits as soon as he enters a room or building, including client homes and job sites. He studies people’s faces and body language to work out whether they might be a threat, and even inside his own house he says he remains on guard. At night he checks the doors and listens carefully when he wakes in the dark. He is a plumber, so these reactions arise repeatedly in other people’s homes, on building sites, and in cramped service spaces where he cannot instantly control who is nearby or where the nearest exit is.

A self-reinforcing pattern is evident. Night-time darkness, bang sounds, and moments of feeling out of control bring the trauma back in fragments and images, which intensify his sense that he is unsafe, isolated, and somehow responsible. Those appraisals sit alongside more scanning, more vigilance around faces and exits, and more guardedness at home and at work. Rajan’s checking keeps the event mentally close, and the repeated return of those pictures and bodily alarms makes the same beliefs feel more convincing. His marriage is affected too: he is physically present but preoccupied and watchful, often distracted from ordinary conversation because he is assessing who is near the door and where he could get out quickly.

violations.explanation: The vignette explicitly describes checking and scanning as a coping pattern: 'In daily life, Rajan scans for exits...' and 'At night he checks the doors...' This presents maladaptive strategies as a response to triggers.
violations.quote: In daily life, Rajan scans for exits as soon as he enters a room or building... At night he checks the doors and listens carefully when he wakes in the dark.

violations.explanation: The narrative links his beliefs to increased checking/vigilance: 'those appraisals sit alongside more scanning, more vigilance around faces and exits, and more guardedness at home and at work.' This is explicit causal direction from negative appraisals to maladaptive strategies.
violations.quote: Those appraisals sit alongside more scanning, more vigilance around faces and exits, and more guardedness at home and at work.

violations.explanation: The vignette states that the return of traumatic pictures and bodily alarms reinforces checking: 'Rajan’s checking keeps the event mentally close.' This is an explicit causal connection from memory to maladaptive strategies.
violations.quote: Rajan’s checking keeps the event mentally close, and the repeated return of those pictures and bodily alarms makes the same beliefs feel more convincing.

violations.explanation: The account says that feeling unsafe leads to further scanning and vigilance: 'those appraisals sit alongside more scanning...' Since 'unsafe' corresponds to threat, this implies threat contributes to maladaptive strategies.
violations.quote: Those appraisals sit alongside more scanning, more vigilance around faces and exits, and more guardedness at home and at work.

violations.explanation: The sentence 'Rajan’s checking keeps the event mentally close' explicitly states that checking maintains the traumatic memory.
violations.quote: Rajan’s checking keeps the event mentally close, and the repeated return of those pictures and bodily alarms makes the same beliefs feel more convincing.

violations.explanation: The vignette explicitly says the checking and repeated return of images 'makes the same beliefs feel more convincing,' which is causal language from maladaptive strategies to negative appraisals.
violations.quote: Rajan’s checking keeps the event mentally close, and the repeated return of those pictures and bodily alarms makes the same beliefs feel more convincing.

violations.explanation: The vignette does contain safety behaviors such as scanning for exits and checking doors, which could resemble maladaptive strategies, so this forbidden edge is not cleanly absent.
violations.quote: “In daily life, Rajan scans for exits as soon as he enters a room or building...”

violations.explanation: The vignette likewise suggests his beliefs may be associated with hypervigilant behavior, so the forbidden causal direction is potentially implied.
violations.quote: “His central stuck point is painfully clear...”

violations.explanation: The trauma memory is linked to his current hypervigilance and checking behaviors, which could be read as caused by the memory.
violations.quote: “The trauma memory stays in his mind...”

violations.explanation: His sense of danger is followed by checking, scanning, and staying on guard, which are avoidant/safety behaviors consistent with maladaptive strategies.
violations.quote: “He studies people’s faces and body language to work out whether they might be a threat... At night he checks the doors...”

violations.explanation: No explicit causal language shows the strategies causing the memory itself.
No explicit causal language shows the strategies causing the negative appraisals.
